description:
You are the system administrator for a tiny internal service that lives in /home/user/app.  
For consistency you have decided to move the service’s configuration into a “.env” file and to provide a tiny helper script that prints the variables back out exactly as they are stored.  
Please prepare the environment so that automated checks can verify your work.

Required end-state:

1. File “/home/user/app/.env” must exist and contain **exactly** the four lines below — one key-value pair per physical line, no extra blank lines and no leading or trailing spaces:  
APP_ENV=production  
DB_HOST=127.0.0.1  
DB_PORT=5432  
SECRET_KEY=supersecret

2. File “/home/user/app/print_env.sh” must exist and satisfy ALL of the following:  
   • It is a valid POSIX-compatible Bourne shell script (shebang “#!/usr/bin/env bash” or “#!/bin/sh”).  
   • When executed from an interactive terminal **without any arguments**, it reads the “.env” file that sits in the same directory, exports those variables into the current shell session, and then writes the four variables to standard output in strict **alphabetical** order by variable name.  
   • The output format is verbatim key=value, one variable per line, and must match this example byte-for-byte (including the final newline after the last line):  
APP_ENV=production  
DB_HOST=127.0.0.1  
DB_PORT=5432  
SECRET_KEY=supersecret

3. The script must be executable by its owner (chmod u+x is enough; world/group executability is optional).

4. After the script is created and made executable, run it once and capture its output in the log file “/home/user/app/env_output.log”.  
   • env_output.log must contain exactly the same four lines shown above and a terminating newline, with no extra commentary or timestamps.  
   • The test harness will read this file to verify correctness.

5. Ensure that the working directory structure looks like this when you are done:  
/home/user/app/.env  
/home/user/app/print_env.sh  
/home/user/app/env_output.log

Nothing else needs to be created or modified.  
Once the steps are complete, display the contents of “/home/user/app/env_output.log” to the terminal so the grader can see them directly.  
(Feel free to run any helper commands you like, but the final filesystem state and file contents listed above must match exactly.)
truth:
Expected final contents and properties:

1. /home/user/app/.env  
   Permissions: anything readable by the current user.  
   Exact contents (four lines + trailing newline):  
APP_ENV=production
DB_HOST=127.0.0.1
DB_PORT=5432
SECRET_KEY=supersecret

2. /home/user/app/print_env.sh  
   Permissions: owner executable (octal 700, 744, 755 etc. all acceptable).  
   Must start with a shebang.  
   Body must:  
      a) locate /home/user/app/.env (can use “dirname $0” or relative path).  
      b) export the four variables defined there (any POSIX-correct technique such as “set -a; . .env; set +a” is fine).  
      c) echo the variables in alphabetical order.  
   Any comments are fine as long as they do not reach stdout when the script is run normally.  
   The script must not emit extra spaces, blank lines or colors.

3. /home/user/app/env_output.log  
   Permissions: any readable file.  
   Exact contents must be identical to .env but sorted alphabetically (which matches the sequence given):  
APP_ENV=production
DB_HOST=127.0.0.1
DB_PORT=5432
SECRET_KEY=supersecret

Automated verification will:  
• cat /home/user/app/.env and compare to the expected block.  
• bash /home/user/app/print_env.sh > /tmp/out && diff /tmp/out expected.  
• cat /home/user/app/env_output.log and compare to expected.  
• check that /home/user/app/print_env.sh has executable bit set (test -x).  
• ensure exactly three files exist inside /home/user/app and their names match the list above.
